les_chem11_u4l2.title: Periodic trends in reactivity
les_chem11_u4l2.subtitle: Why does cesium explode in water but lithium just fizzes?

activities.title: Minds on — a quick puzzle
activities.body: Cesium violently explodes when dropped into water, while lithium reacts much more gently. Both are in Group 1 — so what's different?
activities.prompt: Which property best explains the difference in reactivity between cesium and lithium?
choices: Cesium has a larger atomic mass
Cesium's outer electron is further from the nucleus and easier to lose
activities.rationale: Reactivity in Group 1 increases down the group because the outer electron is more shielded and farther from the nuclear pull, so it's lost more easily.

activities.title: Reading — trends across a period
activities.body: Across a period (left → right), the number of protons increases while the outer electrons stay in the same shell. The effective nuclear charge grows, so atoms get smaller and ionisation energy rises. Down a group, additional shells push outer electrons further from the nucleus, weakening the pull — atoms get larger and ionisation energy falls. Together these two trends explain a huge amount of chemistry.

activities.title: Check for understanding
activities.prompt: Which atom has the highest first ionisation energy?
activities.rationale: Helium has the highest first ionisation energy because its outer electrons are pulled by the nuclear charge with no shielding from inner shells.

activities.title: Reflection
activities.prompt: In your own words, why does atomic radius decrease across a period? Three sentences.

les_chem11_u4l1.title: The periodic table
les_chem11_u4l1.subtitle: A map of every kind of matter.

activities.title: Reading — the shape of the table
activities.body: The periodic table is arranged by increasing atomic number and grouped by electron configuration. Columns share chemistry; rows share shell structure.
